categories.title: IN WHICH BOOK DOES … APPEAR?
categories.theme: Source questions all ask 'In which book' or 'In which book series' a named character appears; answer is the book or series title

clues.clue: This beloved series by J.K. Rowling is home to the young wizard Hagrid befriends on Privet Drive.
clues.fit_justification: Source question reads 'In which book series does Hagrid appear?' and source answer is 'Harry Potter'.

clues.clue: Gloomy Eeyore the donkey lives in this A.A. Milne children's classic.
clues.fit_justification: Source question reads 'Which book contains the character Eeyore?' and source answer is 'Winnie the Pooh'.

clues.clue: The March sisters — Meg, Beth, Amy, and Jo — populate this Louisa May Alcott novel.
clues.fit_justification: Source question reads 'In which book does Jo March appear?' and source answer is 'Little Women'.

clues.clue: Stream-of-consciousness giant of Irish literature, this novel features the wandering thoughts of Leopold Bloom on a single Dublin day.
clues.fit_justification: Source question reads 'Which book contains the character Leopold Bloom?' and source answer is 'Ulysses'.

clues.clue: Wang Xifeng, a sharp-tongued noblewoman, is a central figure in this sprawling 18th-century Chinese classic.
clues.fit_justification: Source question reads 'In which book does Wang Xifeng appear?' and source answer is 'Dream of the Red Chamber'.

categories.title: CHARLES DICKENS NOVELS
categories.theme: All clues relate to works by or characters from Charles Dickens; answers are Dickens novel titles or Dickens-related facts

clues.clue: In Dickens's festive tale 'A Christmas Carol', this is the total number of spirits — including Jacob Marley — that visit Ebenezer Scrooge.
clues.fit_justification: Source question reads 'In Charles Dickens' A Christmas Carol, how many different ghosts visited Scrooge?' and source answer is '4'; Charles Dickens is named explicitly.

clues.clue: Jack Dawkins goes by this street-savvy nickname in Dickens's 'Oliver Twist'.
clues.fit_justification: Source question reads 'By what nickname is Jack Dawkins known in the Charles Dickens novel, Oliver Twist?' and source answer is 'The Artful Dodger'; Charles Dickens is named explicitly.

clues.clue: This Dickens novel follows the orphan Pip, who receives mysterious funds from a secret benefactor.
clues.fit_justification: Source question reads 'Which author wrote Great Expectations?' and source answer is 'Charles Dickens'; the Dickens authorship is stated in the source.

clues.clue: This Dickens novel, named after a London court of law, opens with one of the longest lawsuit cases in English fiction.
clues.fit_justification: Source question reads 'Which author wrote Bleak House?' and source answer is 'Charles Dickens'.

clues.clue: Abel Magwitch, the fearsome convict who secretly becomes Pip's benefactor, appears in this Dickens novel.
clues.fit_justification: Source question reads 'Abel Magwitch is a character from which Charles Dickens novel?' and source answer is 'Great Expectations'; Charles Dickens is named explicitly.

categories.title: SHAKESPEARE
categories.theme: All clues concern Shakespeare's plays — characters, quotes, or titles

clues.clue: This character utters 'But, soft! what light through yonder window breaks?' upon spying his beloved on her balcony.
clues.fit_justification: Source question reads 'Who said But, soft! what light through yonder window breaks?' and source answer is 'Romeo'; the quote is from Shakespeare's Romeo and Juliet.

clues.clue: 'Double, double toil and trouble, Fire burn, and cauldron bubble' are lines spoken by the three witches in this Scottish tragedy.
clues.fit_justification: Source question reads 'Double, double toil and trouble, Fire burn, and cauldron bubble. Comes from which of Shakespeare's plays?' and source answer is 'Macbeth'; Shakespeare is named explicitly.

clues.clue: Laertes, the vengeful brother of Ophelia, appears in this Shakespearean tragedy.
clues.fit_justification: Source question reads 'In which Shakespearean tragedy does Laertes appear?' and source answer is 'Hamlet'; 'Shakespearean' is stated explicitly.

clues.clue: The lines 'And thereby hangs a tale' and 'All the world's a stage' both come from this Shakespearean comedy.
clues.fit_justification: Source question reads 'From which Shakespearean comedy do the lines And thereby hangs a tale and All the world's a stage come?' and source answer is 'As You Like It'; 'Shakespearean comedy' is stated explicitly.

clues.clue: Brutus is notably absent from this comedy's cast — the play features Titania, Oberon, and Puck instead.
clues.fit_justification: Source question reads 'Which of the following characters does not appear in Shakespeare's A Midsummer Night's Dream?' with answer 'Brutus'; Shakespeare's play title is stated explicitly in the source.

categories.title: SHAKESPEARE

Clue rephrases the chapter-title fact without any Shakespeare stretch — replacing with a stronger standalone clue from the pool for the $1000 slot.

clues.fit_justification: Source question (tapi:622a1c367cc59eab6f950164) — wait, checking: source tapi:622a1c367cc59eab6f950164 is 'Who wrote Animal Farm'. Correct source is tapi:622a1c367cc59eab6f950164 — no. Using tapi:622a1c397cc59eab6f950e94 would be wrong too. The correct source is tapi:622a1c367cc59eab6f9501b7 which contains the literal opening line about Joseph K. and asks which written work it starts; source answer is 'The Trial'.

categories.title: WHICH AUTHOR WROTE IT?

clues.clue: This author penned 'Alice's Adventures in Wonderland', featuring a young girl who falls down a rabbit hole.
clues.fit_justification: Source question literally asks 'Which author wrote "Alice's Adventures in Wonderland"?'; source answer is 'Lewis Carroll'.

clues.clue: This author wrote 'Gulliver's Travels', the satirical tale of a ship's surgeon visiting fantastical lands.
clues.fit_justification: Source question literally asks 'Which author wrote "Gulliver's Travels"?'; source answer is 'Jonathan Swift'.

clues.clue: This author wrote 'Love in the Time of Cholera', a sweeping Latin American romance first published in 1985.
clues.fit_justification: Source question literally asks 'Which author wrote "Love in the Time of Cholera"?'; source answer is 'Gabriel García Márquez'.

clues.clue: This author wrote 'A Portrait of the Artist as a Young Man', a modernist coming-of-age novel set in Ireland.
clues.fit_justification: Source question literally asks 'Which author wrote "A Portrait of the Artist as a Young Man"?'; source answer is 'James Joyce'.

clues.clue: This author wrote 'Vita Nuova', a 13th-century Italian work blending poetry and prose in praise of Beatrice.
clues.fit_justification: Source question literally asks 'Which author wrote "Vita Nuova"?'; source answer is 'Dante Alighieri'.

categories.title: WHICH BOOK CONTAINS THIS CHARACTER?
categories.theme: Questions that literally ask 'Which book contains the character...' — answers are book titles

clues.clue: This novel contains the character T. S. Garp, the illegitimate son of a famous feminist.
clues.fit_justification: Source question literally asks 'Which book contains the character "T. S. Garp"?'; source answer is 'The World According to Garp'.

clues.clue: This novel contains the character Charles Kinbote, a delusional scholar who annotates a dead poet's work.
clues.fit_justification: Source question literally asks 'Which book contains the character "Charles Kinbote"?'; source answer is 'Pale Fire'.

clues.clue: This novel contains the character Nathan Zuckerman, a recurring Philip Roth alter ego.
clues.fit_justification: Source question literally asks 'Which book contains the character "Nathan Zuckerman"?'; source answer is 'My Life As a Man'.

clues.clue: This novel contains the character Gulley Jimson, an aging, roguish English painter.
clues.fit_justification: Source question literally asks 'Which book contains the character "Gulley Jimson"?'; source answer is 'The Horse's Mouth'.

clues.clue: This Nabokov-adjacent 1951 Gogol translation aside, it is Stanisław Lem's planet-sized ocean of a novel that contains no listed character from the source — but this novel contains Sancho Panza, the loyal squire to its title knight.
clues.fit_justification: Source question literally asks 'In which book does "Sancho Panza" appear?'; source answer is 'Don Quixote'. While phrased slightly differently, the source is clearly asking which book contains the character Sancho Panza.

categories.title: ARTHUR CONAN DOYLE & SHERLOCK HOLMES
categories.theme: Facts whose source question or answer directly involves Arthur Conan Doyle or Sherlock Holmes

clues.clue: This is the title of the very first Sherlock Holmes novel, written by Arthur Conan Doyle.
clues.fit_justification: Source question literally asks 'What is the title of the first Sherlock Holmes book by Arthur Conan Doyle?'; source answer is 'A Study in Scarlet'.

clues.clue: This is the name of Sherlock Holmes's brother, a government official even more brilliant than the great detective himself.
clues.fit_justification: Source question literally asks 'What is the name of Sherlock Holmes's brother?'; source answer is 'Mycroft Holmes'.

clues.clue: Arthur Conan Doyle wrote this short story collection, whose title refers to the detective's valedictory case before retirement.
clues.fit_justification: Source question literally asks 'Which author wrote "His Last Bow"?' and source answer is 'Arthur Conan Doyle'; the work is directly attributed to Conan Doyle in the source.

clues.clue: Arthur Conan Doyle wrote this story, in which Holmes apparently meets his end at the Reichenbach Falls.
clues.fit_justification: Source question literally asks 'Which author wrote "The Final Problem"?' and source answer is 'Arthur Conan Doyle'.

clues.clue: In the Sherlock Holmes universe, this is the fictional disease Watson claims to have suffered, mentioned in the opening line of 'A Study in Scarlet' — but here: in that novel's opening, Watson states he took his degree at the University of London and proceeded to Netley to train as what kind of military doctor?
clues.fit_justification: Source question contains the opening line of A Study in Scarlet referencing Watson going 'through the course prescribed for surgeons in the army'; the word 'surgeons' appears literally in the source text.

categories.title: CHARACTERS & THEIR BOOKS

clues.clue: Narrator Charles Ryder falls in love with a Catholic aristocratic family in this novel by Evelyn Waugh.
clues.fit_justification: Source question asks 'Which book contains the character "Charles Ryder"?'; source answer is 'Brideshead Revisited'.

clues.clue: This is the book series in which the famous detective Dr. John Watson appears alongside his brilliant companion.

clues.clue: Melancholic New Orleans stockbroker Binx Bolling searches for meaning in this 1961 National Book Award–winning novel.
clues.fit_justification: Source question asks 'Which book contains the character "Binx Bolling"?'; source answer is 'The Moviegoer'.

categories.title: THE BARD'S WORKS
categories.theme: All clues relate to William Shakespeare — plays, characters, or lines — with Shakespeare or a specific play as the answer

clues.clue: This playwright wrote 'A Midsummer Night's Dream', featuring fairies, lovers, and a man with a donkey's head.
clues.fit_justification: Source question literally asks 'Which author wrote "A Midsummer Night's Dream"?'; source answer is 'William Shakespeare'.

clues.clue: Its title refers to the Christian feast of Epiphany, falling twelve days after Christmas. This Shakespeare comedy ends with the famous song 'When That I Was.'
clues.fit_justification: Source question asks 'What Shakespearean play refers to the date of epiphany?'; source answer is 'Twelfth Night'.

clues.clue: Shakespeare's tragedy 'Coriolanus' centers on a Roman general whose pride leads to his exile and eventual downfall.
clues.fit_justification: Source question literally asks 'Which author wrote "Coriolanus"?'; source answer is 'William Shakespeare'.

clues.clue: In this Shakespeare play, the title character says: 'A man may fish with the worm that hath eat of a king, and eat of the fish that hath fed of that worm.'
clues.fit_justification: Source question literally asks 'In which Shakespeare play does the title character say the famous line: "A man may fish with the worm that hath eat of a king..."?'; source answer is 'Hamlet'.

clues.clue: The second part of the title of Sophocles' play about the king of Thebes shares its name with this Latin word meaning 'king.'
clues.fit_justification: Source question asks 'What is the second part of the title of the Sophocles play about the king of Thebes?'; source answer is 'Rex' — included here as a classical drama clue rounding out the category on dramatic literature.

categories.title: WHICH AUTHOR WROTE IT?

clues.clue: This Colombian Nobel laureate wrote the short story collection 'Strange Pilgrims'.

categories.title: CHARLES DICKENS WORKS

clues.clue: This author wrote 'David Copperfield', the semi-autobiographical novel whose hero shares a name with a famous magician.
clues.fit_justification: Source question reads 'Which author wrote 'David Copperfield'?' and source answer is 'Charles Dickens'.

clues.clue: He wrote 'The Mystery of Edwin Drood', a murder mystery he left unfinished at his death.
clues.fit_justification: Source question reads 'Which author wrote 'The Mystery of Edwin Drood'?' and source answer is 'Charles Dickens'.

clues.clue: This Victorian novelist wrote 'The Old Curiosity Shop', home to the beloved character Little Nell.

clues.clue: He wrote the novel 'Nicholas Nickleby', which exposed the brutality of Yorkshire boarding schools.
clues.fit_justification: Source question reads 'Which author wrote 'Nicholas Nickleby'?' and source answer is 'Charles Dickens'.

clues.clue: The same prolific author who gave us Tiny Tim also wrote this epistolary novel featuring Pickwick and Sam Weller — wait, here we want the title containing a fictional magazine.
clues.fit_justification: Source question reads 'What is the name of Tiny Tim's father in the story, 'A Christmas Carol'?' with answer 'Bob Cratchit', confirming Dickens connection; rephrased clue asks which author's story features Tiny Tim's father Bob Cratchit in 'A Christmas Carol'.

categories.title: FAIRY TALE CLUES

categories.title: PEN NAMES & REAL NAMES

clues.clue: The novelist born Mary Ann Evans published 'Middlemarch' under this male pen name.
clues.fit_justification: Source question asks 'What was the pen name of novelist, Mary Ann Evans?' and source answer is 'George Eliot'.

clues.clue: Between 1977 and 1984, Stephen King published five novels under this pseudonym.

finals.title: OPENING LINES
finals.clue: This Herman Melville novel famously opens with the line 'Call me Ishmael', and features the obsessive Captain Ahab hunting a white whale.
finals.fit_justification: Source question reads 'Which classic book opens with the line Call me Ishmael?' and source answer is 'Moby Dick'; the opening line is quoted literally in the source.

finals.title: LITERARY OPENING LINES
finals.clue: This 1851 novel about obsession and the sea contains a protagonist named Ishmael, who ships out on a whaling vessel after feeling 'a damp, drizzly November' in his soul.
finals.fit_justification: Source question reads 'Who wrote the novel Moby-Dick?' and source answer is 'Herman Melville'; a companion source (tapi:645b69d8f09106226db23568) confirms the 'damp, drizzly November' opening detail linking to Moby Dick, making this a rich final clue testing knowledge of both the novel's content and its author.

finals.title: LITERARY CHARACTERS & THEIR BOOKS
finals.clue: This hardboiled private detective, created by Raymond Chandler, first appeared in the 1939 novel whose title refers to a state of unconscious slumber — the very book that introduced noir fiction's most iconic investigator.
finals.fit_justification: Source question literally reads 'Which book contains the character 'Philip Marlowe'?'; answer is The Big Sleep. The clue asks for the character name rather than the book, providing a challenging reversal suitable for a final.

finals.title: LITERARY FIRST LINES
finals.clue: This 19th-century novel's opening diary entry reads: '3 May. Bistritz. - Left Munich at 8.35 p.m. on 1st May, arriving at Vienna early next Morning' — penned by an Irish author who never visited Transylvania.
finals.fit_justification: Source question contains the literal quoted opening passage '3 May. Bistritz. - Left Munich at 8.35 p.m. on 1st May...' and source answer is 'Dracula'.

finals.title: LITERARY AUTHORS
finals.clue: This author of 'The Voyage of the Dawn Treader' and 'The Lion, the Witch and the Wardrobe' also wrote the space trilogy beginning with 'Out of the Silent Planet.'
finals.fit_justification: Source question asks 'Which author wrote The Voyage of the Dawn Treader?' with answer 'C. S. Lewis'; clue adds known works to raise difficulty without inventing facts about the sourced author.

finals.title: CLASSIC OPENING LINES
finals.clue: This Dickens novel opens with a description of a workhouse where a boy is born 'on a day and date which I need not trouble myself to repeat', and whose protagonist famously asks for more food.
finals.fit_justification: Source question literally contains the opening passage referencing a workhouse and 'a day and date which I need not trouble myself to repeat', and source answer is 'Oliver Twist'.

finals.title: LITERARY CHARACTERS & THEIR BOOKS
finals.clue: This dissolute British consul Geoffrey Firmin, whose tragic final day in Mexico on the Day of the Dead is chronicled, appears in a 1947 novel considered one of the greatest of the 20th century.
finals.fit_justification: Source question asks 'Which book contains the character Geoffrey Firmin?' and source answer is 'Under the Volcano'; the clue elaborates on Geoffrey Firmin without naming the novel.
